description: Cold archive, rarely queried (~5/month), append-only, large well-sized files. Correct answer: Z (do nothing or minimal), F (periodic snapshot expiry). No compaction, no sort, no z-order.

interview_answers.query_latency_requirement: batch-tolerant — minutes or more is fine, nobody is waiting
interview_answers.query_frequency: almost never — maybe 3–5 times per year for audit purposes
interview_answers.freshness_sla: once-a-day or slower is fine, no real-time requirement
interview_answers.cost_priority: storage cost — minimize the monthly storage bill
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: append-only forever, no updates or deletes
interview_answers.time_travel_need: only the latest state matters, snapshot history is just storage cost
interview_answers.lifecycle: cold archive — rarely touched, optimize for cheap storage

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is to do nothing or at most expire old snapshots (snapshot expiry / expire_snapshots). Because the table is a cold archive with only 3–5 queries per year and files are already well-sized at 300 MB, data file compaction (rewrite_data_files / optimize) would cost more compute than it ever saves. Sort compaction, z-order, bloom filters, and partition evolution are all wasteful at this query volume. Only snapshot expiry is justified if there are many stale snapshots. A recommendation to run compaction, sort, or z-order is incorrect.

description: Streaming table, 30-second commit cadence, 1 MB files spread across 20 partitions per commit (thin spread). Queried constantly by tenant_id + event_time for interactive dashboards. Correct: A (bin-pack cold partitions), C (z-order tenant_id + event_time), J (write-time tuning distribution-mode=hash), F (aggressive snapshot expiry).

interview_answers.query_latency_requirement: interactive — sub-second to seconds, user-facing dashboards
interview_answers.query_frequency: constantly — hundreds of queries per hour from many consumers
interview_answers.freshness_sla: minutes is acceptable — 5-minute delay is fine
interview_answers.cost_priority: query latency — we optimize for speed, not cost
interview_answers.time_travel_need: only the latest state matters, history is exhaust
interview_answers.lifecycle: hot — actively used, optimize for speed

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is to compact the 1 MB small files (bin-pack or sort compaction via rewrite_data_files), apply z-order or sort on tenant_id + event_time to enable data skipping, tune write-time distribution (distribution-mode=hash or similar) to prevent future thin-spread small files, and expire snapshots aggressively. The table has 5,000 small 1 MB files spread thin — compaction is urgent. A recommendation that skips compaction or says 'do nothing' is incorrect.

description: Table with accumulating equality deletes from GDPR right-to-be-forgotten purges (~1M records/day). eq_delete_pressure = 10%. Queried regularly by compliance team. Correct: E1 (equality delete compaction with remove-dangling-deletes, non-optional), F (aggressive snapshot expiry to physically remove data). Must mention physical deletion and snapshot history risk.

interview_answers.query_latency_requirement: responsive — seconds to minutes is acceptable
interview_answers.query_frequency: regularly — daily reports by the compliance team
interview_answers.freshness_sla: hours is fine — same-day is acceptable
interview_answers.cost_priority: query cost — minimize scan compute
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: deletes required — GDPR right-to-be-forgotten, individual row deletions on regulatory deadlines
interview_answers.time_travel_need: only the latest state matters — retained snapshots are a GDPR liability, not a feature
interview_answers.retention_policy: GDPR right-to-be-forgotten — must physically remove rows within 30 days of request

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must include: (1) rewrite_data_files with remove-dangling-deletes to physically remove deleted rows from data files — this is non-optional for GDPR compliance; (2) expire_snapshots aggressively (preferably retain_last=1 or near-current only) because retained snapshot history means deleted rows can still be read from older snapshots, creating a GDPR liability. The recommendation must explicitly warn that logical delete files are not enough for compliance — physical removal requires both compaction and snapshot expiry. A recommendation that omits compaction or omits snapshot expiry, or that does not mention the GDPR/compliance risk of snapshot history, is incorrect.

description: Well-maintained table partitioned by event_date (daily). But 98% of queries filter on tenant_id only — partition_prune_rate = 0.0. Every query scans the full 12.5 GB table. Profile looks healthy (256 MB files, no deletes, no small files). The problem is invisible without the workload. Correct: D (partition evolution to bucket/hash by tenant_id) or B/C (sort/z-order by tenant_id as a cheaper alternative). Must identify tenant_id as the key column to optimize on.

interview_answers.query_frequency: constantly — 1000+ queries per month from analysts
interview_answers.cost_priority: query cost — the query bills are getting large

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is to fix the partition-column mismatch. Queries filter almost exclusively on tenant_id, but the table is partitioned by event_date — so partition pruning never fires (prune_rate=0.0) and every query full-table scans 12.5 GB. The recommendation must identify tenant_id as the key column to optimize on, and propose either: (a) partition evolution to bucket or hash-partition by tenant_id (strongest fix, eliminates full scans), or (b) sort compaction / z-order by tenant_id (cheaper alternative, enables data skipping without re-partitioning). A recommendation that ignores tenant_id, focuses only on the file sizes (which are already healthy), or recommends snapshot expiry as the main action is incorrect.

description: Small table with 5 well-sized 256 MB files but 1200 hourly snapshots (1+ year of history). No small files, no deletes. Queried weekly for reports with good partition pruning. Correct: F (snapshot expiry — the only real problem), G (manifest rewrite). Must NOT recommend data file compaction (files are already well-sized).

interview_answers.query_latency_requirement: batch-tolerant — weekly reports, minutes is fine
interview_answers.cost_priority: storage cost — minimize storage bill
interview_answers.time_travel_need: nice to have — keep 2 weeks of history for rollback

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is snapshot expiry (expire_snapshots / expire_snapshots with retention_threshold) as the primary action — the table has 1,200 hourly snapshots accumulated over more than a year, causing metadata bloat. Optionally also rewrite manifests (optimize_manifests). The data files themselves are perfectly sized at 256 MB — data file compaction (rewrite_data_files / EXECUTE optimize) is not needed and would waste compute. A recommendation that prescribes data file compaction, sort, or z-order as a main action is incorrect; the files are already well-sized.

description: Merge-on-read table with 240 position delete files accumulating against 200 data files (pos_delete_pressure=0.15). 280 merge commits in 10 weeks. Queried 300x/month by customer_id + updated_at range. Correct: E2 (rewrite_position_delete_files, not E1), conservative snapshot expiry retaining ≥7 days, and a note that COW should be considered long-term if delete rate continues.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query cost — minimize scan compute
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: frequent updates — CDC merge-on-read pattern, ~3.2 delete commits per day
interview_answers.time_travel_need: important — keep at least 7 days for rollback

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is position-delete compaction using rewrite_position_delete_files (or rewrite_data_files targeting files with position deletes) — NOT equality-delete compaction (E1), because the delete files are position deletes (merge-on-read pattern) not equality deletes. Snapshot expiry should be conservative, retaining at least 7 days (not aggressive like GDPR tables). The recommendation should mention considering copy-on-write mode as a long-term alternative if the delete rate continues to grow. A recommendation that prescribes equality-delete compaction (E1 / remove-dangling-deletes) as the primary action, or one that recommends aggressive snapshot expiry, is incorrect.

description: v1 Iceberg table with accumulating equality deletes from a CDC pipeline. The table must be upgraded to v2 before any equality-delete compaction is safe. Correct: L first (ALTER TABLE SET 'format-version'='2'), then E1. A v1 table cannot safely compact equality delete files.

interview_answers.freshness_sla: minutes acceptable — 5-minute CDC lag is fine
interview_answers.cost_priority: query cost — equality deletes are hurting scan performance
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: frequent updates and deletes — CDC equality delete pattern

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must identify that the table is still Iceberg format version 1, which does not support deletion vectors or safe equality-delete compaction. The mandatory first step is to upgrade to format version 2 using ALTER TABLE SET TBLPROPERTIES ('format-version'='2'). Only after upgrading to v2 can rewrite_data_files with remove-dangling-deletes (E1) be safely run. A recommendation that immediately runs E1/equality-delete compaction without first upgrading the format version is incorrect and potentially unsafe. The format version upgrade (Action L) must be explicitly mentioned as the first step.

description: Table with a 5-level partition spec (year/month/day/hour/region) creating ~219,000 partitions. Average 2 files per partition, 8 MB each. Total 250 GB across 437k tiny files. Massive metadata overhead. Correct: D (partition evolution — drop hour and day dimensions, keep year/month/region or bucket).

interview_answers.cost_priority: query cost — planning overhead is enormous

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is partition evolution (Action D) — specifically, reducing the over-granular partition spec. The 5-level hierarchy (year/month/day/hour/region) creates approximately 219,000 partitions with only 2 files each (8 MB average), causing severe metadata and planning overhead. The fix is to evolve the partition spec to drop the hour and day dimensions, keeping a coarser granularity like year/month/region or introducing a bucket on a high-cardinality column. Bin-pack (A) or sort (B) alone is not the primary fix — they are band-aids that don't address the root cause of too many tiny partitions. A recommendation that only recommends compaction without addressing the partition spec is incomplete.

description: Flink streaming table, 30-second commits, 0.5 MB files scattered across all partitions per commit (extreme thin spread). 50 partitions touched per commit. Queried 600x/month by event_type + region. Correct: J (distribution-mode=hash on event_type), K (write-time sort by event_type, region), A (bin-pack cold partitions), G (manifest clustering). Must NOT recommend z-order.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query latency — speed is paramount

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is to fix the root cause at write time: set distribution-mode=hash on event_type so Flink routes each event type to a dedicated writer task (eliminating thin spread), and set a write-time sort order (event_type, region) so files are pre-sorted. Then run bin-pack compaction on cold partitions and manifest clustering (G) to reduce metadata overhead. Z-order is NOT recommended here — the table has two low-to-medium cardinality columns (event_type, region) and the thin spread problem is a writer-side issue, not a sort order issue. A recommendation that prescribes z-order without addressing distribution-mode is missing the root cause.

description: Batch ETL table partitioned by event_date. Most data arrives on time but ~5% of events arrive 7–30 days late, rewriting old partitions. Write-time sort order already set (event_date, customer_id). But late-arriving rewrites undo previous sort. Correct: B (sort compaction triggered on partitions modified within last 30 days), not adding K again (already set).

interview_answers.cost_priority: query cost — late arriving data is hurting scan performance
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: append-mostly with late-arriving data — 5% of events arrive 7–30 days late

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is to run sort compaction (rewrite_data_files with sort strategy) on partitions that were recently modified (last 7–30 days) to restore the sort order after late-arriving data rewrites. The write-time sort order is already set (event_date, customer_id), so Action K should NOT be recommended again — it's already in place and doesn't help with the late-arriving data problem. The fix is periodic sort compaction that targets recently modified partitions. A recommendation that adds write-time sort order again (K), or that recommends z-order instead of sort compaction, is incorrect.

description: 250-column wide table with 512 MB target file size (default). Queries on 600 GB table by customer_id + order_date. Compaction workers are OOMing. Files are already 512 MB (at target) so the profiler says no binpack needed — but the target itself is wrong for a wide schema. Correct: Reduce write.target-file-size-bytes to 128–256 MB, then sort compaction.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query latency — speed is paramount

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must identify that the 512 MB target file size is too large for a 250-column wide table and is causing memory pressure (OOM) during compaction. The primary fix is to reduce write.target-file-size-bytes to 128–256 MB to bring memory usage within the executor budget. After re-sizing, sort compaction on (customer_id, order_date) can proceed safely. A recommendation that only recommends compaction without addressing the file size configuration, or that says 'no action needed because files are already at target size', is incorrect.

description: MOR table with CDC churning 200k updates/hour producing 12 MB average write files. Equality deletes at 8% pressure. 1000 queries/month by account_id equality + event_date range. Query latency is top priority. Correct: E1 (equality-delete compaction) + J (write-time tuning for 12 MB small write files, target 256 MB) + A (bin-pack) + consideration of switching to COW mode given latency priority.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query latency — interactive dashboards require sub-second
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: frequent updates — CDC pipeline, 200k updates per hour

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation includes: (1) equality-delete compaction (rewrite_data_files with remove-dangling-deletes / E1) to remove the accumulating equality deletes; (2) write-time tuning (Action J) to address the 12 MB small write files — the CDC writer is producing files far below the 256 MB target, so distribution-mode or target-file-size tuning is needed; (3) bin-pack compaction (A) to merge the small write files; and (4) a note that switching to copy-on-write mode should be evaluated since query latency is the top priority and the table has frequent update churn. A recommendation that omits the write-time tuning (J) or that doesn't mention COW mode as a consideration when latency is paramount, is incomplete.

description: Cold archive with 3 queries per year, well-sized 512 MB files, no deletes, no small files. Compaction would cost $200 in compute. Lifetime query savings from compaction: ~$100. Correct: Z (explicit do-nothing for data files) + periodic snapshot expiry only. Cost of compaction exceeds lifetime benefit.

interview_answers.query_latency_requirement: batch-tolerant — hours is fine, audit queries only
interview_answers.query_frequency: almost never — 3 times per year at most
interview_answers.cost_priority: storage cost — we just want it to be cheap to keep around
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: append-only — no new data, fully historical

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation is to do nothing for data files (explicit do-nothing / Action Z). With only 3 queries per year and files already well-sized at 512 MB, compaction would cost more in compute than it would ever save in query cost — the math doesn't work out. Snapshot expiry is the only justified maintenance action if there is accumulated snapshot history. A recommendation that prescribes data file compaction, sort, z-order, or bloom filters for a table queried 3 times per year is wasteful and incorrect. The key insight is that maintenance has a cost too, and for nearly-dead tables the break-even point is never reached.

description: CDC table with 1 commit per minute (525,600 snapshots/year). Time travel / rollback is required for 30 days. Using retain_last=100 which only keeps ~100 minutes. Must use time-based max-snapshot-age-ms=2592000000 (30 days) instead. Correct: F with time-based expiry, NOT count-based retain_last.

interview_answers.cost_priority: balanced — both cost and latency matter
interview_answers.mutability_outlook: frequent updates — CDC pipeline at 1 commit per minute
interview_answers.time_travel_need: critical — 30-day rollback window required by compliance

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must identify that retain_last=100 with a 1-commit-per-minute table only retains 100 minutes of history — catastrophically short of the required 30-day rollback window. The fix is to switch to time-based snapshot expiry using max-snapshot-age-ms=2592000000 (30 days in milliseconds), not count-based retain_last. A recommendation that continues using retain_last without addressing the 100-minute window problem, or that recommends retain_last with a larger number without converting to time-based, is missing the fundamental issue. The skill must explicitly recommend time-based expiry and explain why count-based is wrong for high-frequency commit tables.

description: Table with two active partition specs after a schema evolution: old spec (year, month) covers 600 GB of historical data; new spec (year, month, bucket(10, tenant_id)) covers 200 GB of recent data. Planning overhead is severe because Iceberg must handle both specs simultaneously. Correct: D (partition evolution) + full rewrite of old-spec data to migrate to new spec.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query cost — planning is too slow

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must address the mixed partition specs — historical data under the old (year, month) spec cannot benefit from the new bucket(10, tenant_id) partitioning, causing split planning. The fix requires rewriting the 600 GB of old-spec data files to the new partition spec (not just a manifest rewrite, which does not move data). This is a full data rewrite triggered by partition evolution. A recommendation that only rewrites manifests (G) without physically rewriting the data files is incorrect — manifests record which spec each file belongs to but cannot change the physical file layout.

description: Table queried primarily by product_id equality (10 million distinct values). Min/max statistics are useless for high-cardinality equality lookups. No bloom filter currently set. Correct: I (enable bloom filter on product_id). Min/max alone cannot prune files for equality on 10M distinct values.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query latency — speed is critical

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must identify that min/max column statistics are ineffective for equality filtering on product_id with 10 million distinct values — with such high cardinality, every file will have overlapping min/max ranges and no file pruning occurs. The fix is to enable a bloom filter on product_id (Action I). The bloom filter allows Iceberg to skip data files that definitely don't contain the queried product_id with a very low false-positive rate. A recommendation that only recommends sort compaction or z-order without mentioning bloom filters for this high-cardinality equality lookup pattern is missing the most impactful fix.

description: GDPR table where snapshots were expired BEFORE equality-delete compaction ran. Equality delete files still present (8% pressure) but the snapshots they reference have been purged. Deleted rows may still be readable via the equality delete files. Correct: urgently run E1 (rewrite_data_files with remove-dangling-deletes) NOW to physically remove deleted rows. Correct order: compact THEN expire.

interview_answers.cost_priority: compliance — GDPR physical deletion is non-negotiable
interview_answers.time_travel_need: none — snapshots should have been expired already, but weren't at the right time

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must urgently prescribe equality-delete compaction (rewrite_data_files with remove-dangling-deletes / E1) as an immediate action. The root cause is that snapshots were expired before compaction ran — the correct order is: (1) compact data files to physically embed/remove deleted rows, THEN (2) expire snapshots. Since snapshots were already expired before compaction, the deleted rows may still be physically present in the data files (as equality delete files that haven't been merged in). Running E1 immediately is the remediation. The recommendation must also explain the correct order: compact THEN expire for GDPR tables. A recommendation that says nothing about the incorrect expiry ordering, or that prescribes only snapshot expiry without urgent E1 compaction, is incorrect.

description: Table with z-order applied to 6 columns (tenant_id, event_time, region, product_id, channel, device_type). Z-curve locality degrades beyond 4-5 columns — in 6D z-space all file ranges overlap and no skipping occurs. High compaction cost with near-zero query benefit. Correct: Replace 6-column z-order with 2-column z-order (tenant_id, event_time) or plain sort.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query latency — minimize scan latency

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must identify that z-order on 6 columns loses locality guarantees — the Z-curve space is too high-dimensional (6 columns) and all file min/max ranges overlap, rendering data skipping ineffective. The fix is to replace the 6-column z-order with a 2-column z-order on (tenant_id, event_time) only, or switch to a simple sort order on the top-2 query columns. Columns like channel, device_type, and region should be dropped from the z-order specification. A recommendation that keeps the same 6-column z-order, or one that recommends adding more columns, is incorrect.

description: Streaming table with hourly compaction job. Compaction runs on today's active partition — exactly where Flink is currently writing. Optimistic concurrency conflicts abort compaction 80% of the time. Correct: Add WHERE event_date < current_date() to compaction to target only cold (yesterday and older) partitions.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query latency — minimize scan latency

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must identify the hot partition conflict: the compaction job is targeting the currently-active partition (today's date partition) while Flink is simultaneously writing to it, causing optimistic concurrency conflicts that abort the compaction job. The fix is to add a filter to the compaction call so it only targets cold (already-closed) partitions — typically WHERE event_date < current_date() or equivalent. Compaction should only run on partitions where writing has completed. A recommendation that suggests disabling compaction, reducing compaction frequency, or increasing concurrency retries without addressing the root cause (hot partition targeting) is incorrect.

description: Table with orphan file cleanup (remove_orphan_files) scheduled BEFORE snapshot expiry. This is the wrong order — snapshot expiry must run first to dereference files, then orphan cleanup identifies which files are no longer referenced. Running orphan cleanup before expiry risks classifying still-valid snapshot files as orphans. Correct: Always expire_snapshots FIRST, then remove_orphan_files with dry_run=true.

interview_answers.cost_priority: storage cost — reduce storage footprint

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must identify the incorrect maintenance ordering: orphan file removal must always come AFTER snapshot expiry, not before. The reason is that snapshot expiry dereferences files by removing snapshot metadata — only after expiry does Iceberg know which files are no longer referenced by any live snapshot. If orphan cleanup runs before expiry, it sees files that are still referenced by to-be-expired snapshots and may incorrectly classify them or miss them. The correct order is: (1) expire_snapshots to dereference files, (2) remove_orphan_files with dry_run=true and a ≥3-day safety margin. A recommendation that continues the current wrong order, or that doesn't mention the ordering requirement, is incorrect.

description: Table with a bloom filter incorrectly configured on event_date (only 365 distinct values, queried with range predicates). Bloom filters require high-cardinality equality columns — they are useless for range predicates and wasteful for low-cardinality columns. Correct: Remove the bloom filter from event_date. Optionally add bloom filter to a high-cardinality equality column if one exists.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query cost — reduce scan overhead

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must identify that the bloom filter on event_date is misconfigured and wasteful: event_date has only 365 distinct values (low cardinality) and is queried with range predicates (BETWEEN, >=, <=). Bloom filters are only useful for high-cardinality equality lookups (e.g., user_id='abc123') — they cannot answer range queries and with only 365 distinct values the false-positive rate is high. The recommendation must say to remove the bloom filter from event_date. For range predicates, min/max statistics (already maintained automatically) are the correct pruning mechanism. A recommendation that keeps the bloom filter on event_date, or that adds more bloom filters without addressing the wrong column, is incorrect.

description: EMERGENCY: Streaming table where compaction has fallen irreversibly behind ingest. New data arriving at 500 MB/min creates files faster than the nightly compaction can process (running 8 hours on 2 TB already). Files: 180k files, 1.2 MB avg. Correct: EMERGENCY fix write-time (distribution-mode=hash, target-file-size-bytes=256MB) FIRST to stop the bleed, then emergency bin-pack on cold partitions only. Without write-time fix, compaction can never catch up.

interview_answers.cost_priority: query latency — system is degrading, need immediate fix
interview_answers.lifecycle: hot — critical production streaming table in crisis

expected_outcome: The correct recommendation must treat this as an emergency and prescribe a two-phase fix: FIRST, fix write-time configuration (distribution-mode=hash + target-file-size-bytes=256MB) to stop the ingest from generating small files — without this fix, compaction is running on a treadmill and will never catch up. SECOND, run emergency bin-pack compaction on cold partitions only (past days/weeks) to reduce the existing file count backlog, while the write-time fix prevents new small files from being created. The recommendation must explicitly state that write-time fix MUST come first, and that compaction alone (without write-time fix) cannot solve the spiral. A recommendation that only recommends increasing compaction frequency without fixing write-time configuration is incorrect.
